text.title: Cloud Provider Selection Decision Tree Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the decision-tree-cloud-provider methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with sourced rationale; the final rule is the skip-this-methodology gate referenced by the decision tree.

rule.statement: When the team already owns Microsoft AD / Office 365, Azure MUST be the default unless a specific workload disqualifies it. When the team owns Google Workspace / BigQuery, GCP MUST be the default. Ignoring existing investments wastes BYOL discounts and identity-migration effort.

rationale.source: Gartner 2024 cloud-migration cost study; Microsoft BYOL guidance.

rule.rationale: Switching identity providers (Active Directory ↔ Google Workspace) or analytics platforms (BigQuery ↔ Redshift) costs significantly more than the marginal service price difference; existing investments create the strongest gravitational pull.

rule.statement: When existing investments do not bind, route by workload-type strength: enterprise breadth → AWS, hybrid on-prem → Azure, analytics / ML / Kubernetes → GCP. The workload-fit branch MUST be applied only when r1 is neutral.
rule.rationale: Each hyperscaler retains a workload niche where its managed services are demonstrably cheaper or faster to ship (BigQuery for analytics, Azure Arc for hybrid, AWS for broadest service catalog).

rule.statement: Multi-cloud MUST NOT be selected as 'best practice'. It is justified only by an explicit, documented goal (regulatory DR, best-of-breed for a single workload). For teams under 50 engineers, multi-cloud doubles identity + networking + cost-management surface.
rule.rationale: Operational overhead of multi-cloud (two control planes, two IAM models, two billing engines) consumes engineering capacity the team rarely has.

rule.statement: Hard compliance requirements (HIPAA, FedRAMP High, EU sovereign cloud) MUST be checked BEFORE workload-fit. A provider missing a required certification is eliminated immediately, regardless of price or workload fit.
rule.rationale: Compliance non-conformance is unrecoverable post-deployment; cost and workload-fit can be retuned, but a missing certification forces full re-architecture.

rule.statement: Every cloud-provider ADR MUST include a runner-up provider and a documented trigger that would cause re-evaluation (e.g. egress cost > $X/month, new compliance regime). The decision is reviewed annually.
rule.rationale: Provider markets shift fast (egress price changes, new sovereign regions); without a recorded runner-up + trigger, re-evaluation is painful and is therefore skipped.

rule.statement: When prerequisites hold (existing investments mapped, workload profile written, compliance matrix complete, cost envelope known), walk the tree r1 → r4 → r2 → r5 and emit the ADR.
rule.rationale: Partial input produces partial output and re-litigation; the checklist is the gate.

rule.statement: Do NOT use when (a) a mandate already pins the provider, (b) deployment is cloud-agnostic, (c) the choice is a single managed service. Emit a short note recording the constraint and exit.
rule.rationale: Forcing the tree under these constraints adds ceremony without changing the answer.
